retrieved_docs.title: Octreotide -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Octreotide is a medication used in the management and treatment of acromegaly and thyrotrophinomas. It is in the somatostatin analog class of drugs. This activity describes the indications, action, and contraindications for octreotide as a valuable agent in the management of acromegaly and thyrotrophinomas, as well as carcinoid syndrome. This activity also highlights the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors (e.g., off-label uses, dosing, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, monitoring, relevant interactions) pertinent for members of the interprofessional team in the management of patients with endocrinopathies and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Octreotide -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Octreotide is an analog of the polypeptide hormone somatostatin. [1] Octreotide acts on the somatostatin receptors, which couple to phospholipase C via inhibitory G proteins, and causes vascular smooth muscle contraction. The alpha and beta-gamma subunits of the G proteins inhibit adenyl cyclase and stimulate phospholipase C, respectively. At a cellular level, like somatostatin, octreotide induces an increase in calcium entry via L-type calcium channels, which leads to increased calcium-induced calcium release via ryanodine receptor calcium release channels from the sarcoplasmic reticulum in the smooth muscle cells, thus, activating myosin light-chain kinase via its interaction with calcium-calmodulin, and therefore, initiating the contraction cycle. The release of calcium from the sarcoplasmic reticulum is also due to the formation of 1, 4,5-inositol triphosphate, potentiated by phospholipase C. [6] Hence, octreotide, similar to the action of endogenous somatostatin, inhibits the release of hormones from the anterior pituitary gland, including thyroid-stimulating hormone and growth hormone, and hormones of the gastroenteropancreatic endocrine system such as insulin and glucagon. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Chylous Ascites(Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Medical Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Somatostatin and the synthetic analog octreotide have been used in the treatment of CA. The exact mechanism of action is not completely understood. It can decrease the portal pressure (by inhibiting splanchnic vasodilation), peristalsis, intestinal absorption of fats, triglyceride concentration in the thoracic duct, and the lymph flow in the major channels. The route of administration and the half-life are different between somatostatin and octreotide. Somatostatins are administered intravenously and have a half-life of 1 to 3 minutes, while octreotide is administered subcutaneously and has a half-life of about 2 hours. [18] Octreotide has successfully been used in patients with CA secondary to pancreatitis, post-liver transplant, malignancy, portal vein thrombosis, and idiopathic causes, improving their quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroendocrine Tumor 177Lutetium-Dotatate Therapy -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA approved 177 Lu-dotatate therapy in 2018 to treat somatostatin receptor (SSTR) positive gastroenteropancreatic NETs. Most NETs show relatively high expression levels of SSTRs, which allows imaging and therapy using radiolabeled somatostatin analogs. 177 Lu is a commonly used radionucleotide for targeted radiation therapy. The combination of radionucleotide 177 Lu with the somatostatin analog dotatate delivers ionizing radiation that targets tumor cells expressing somatostatin receptors, causing radiation-induced single- and double-stranded DNA breaks that lead to apoptosis. This action is more specific for somatostatin receptor-positive lesions. [16] Based on recent clinical studies, this treatment has yielded a significant response rate, accompanied by substantial symptomatic improvement. [17] However, long-term effects include renal dysfunction, pancytopenia, and myelodysplastic syndrome. This treatment is commonly indicated for well-differentiated tumors with relatively high expression of somatostatin receptors. 177 Lu-dotatate is the treatment of choice for well-differentiated NETs, especially those that progress despite using long-acting somatostatin analogs. This treatment is favored over monitoring or administering targeted therapies (eg, everolimus or sunitinib).

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroendocrine Tumor 177Lutetium-Dotatate Therapy -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: 177 Lu-dotatate is administered as an intravenous infusion in an outpatient setting. The recommended dose is 7.4 GBq (200 mCi) every 8 weeks for 4 doses. Dose adjustments may be needed based on patients' renal and hematological parameters. Long-acting somatostatin analogs will be discontinued for at least 4 weeks before initiating this therapy. However, short-acting octreotide is prescribed as needed for symptom control. After treatment, long-acting somatostatin analogs are continued every 4 weeks until the disease progresses. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Octreotide -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Although it has been a few decades since the synthesis of octreotide, its use has limitations to a few indications. [10] However, in recent times, octreotide has found utility in various other previously unexplored therapeutic modalities. Although the understanding of octreotide is increasing due to the numerous studies and clinical trials, further reviews and trials are required to better understand the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of the drug to recognize better and control the adverse effects of octreotide. Also, there is a need for coordination amongst the healthcare team members to enhance evidence-based outcomes for patients on octreotide therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroendocrine Tumor 177Lutetium-Dotatate Therapy -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: 177 Lu-dotatate therapy is a form of endo-radiotherapy that combines a radionuclide with a peptide that specifically binds to peptide receptors. The high affinity of these somatostatin receptors (SSTRs) causes the internalization of the receptor–peptide complex through endocytosis. These peptide receptors (ie, somatostatin receptors) are expressed at high concentrations on tumor cells as assessed by 68 Ga positron emission tomography (PET) scans. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Octreotide Scan -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Octreotide is radiolabeled with Indium-111 ( 111 In) for imaging purposes. This radiolabeled tracer binds to tumor cells that express somatostatin receptors (SSTRs). Somatostatin is secreted in 2 forms in the body, consisting of 14 and 28 amino acids, which result from differential proteolytic processing of a single precursor. Both forms can activate SSTRs at nanomolar concentrations, as they both contain the peptide region critical for receptor interaction. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroendocrine Tumor 177Lutetium-Dotatate Therapy -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: This treatment also has an impact on the quality of life. A study concluded that the time to deterioration in the quality of life was significantly longer with 177 Lu-dotatate compared with octreotide in several domains, including global health status, physical functioning, role functioning, fatigue, pain, diarrhea, disease-related worries, and body image. The differences were clinically significant in global health status, with median times to deterioration in quality of life of 28.8 months versus 6.1 months and physical functioning of 25.2 months versus 11.5 months. [20] In a later analysis, 5 years after the last patient, long-term outcomes were randomized with a median follow-up of 76 months in both groups. [21] [ 55 ] Notably, 12% of the 177 patients in the 177 Lu-dotatate group received further PRRT, whereas 36% of patients in the control group had a crossover to 177 Lu-dotatate. The final overall survival in the intent-to-treat population was not significantly different between the study groups (HR, 0.84; 95% CI, 0.60-1.17), likely due to the crossover design.

retrieved_docs.title: Octreotide Scan -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Patients receiving octreotide or other somatostatin therapies should discontinue treatment before the scan. Short-acting somatostatin analogues should ideally be stopped 24 hours before the 111 In-pentetreotide injection, or at least 12 hours prior. This requirement may be waived if the patient's clinical condition necessitates continued therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroendocrine Tumor 177Lutetium-Dotatate Therapy -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The global phase 3 NETTER-1 trial is the most compelling evidence of clinical benefit for 177 Lu-dotatate in midgut GINETs. In this study, 229 patients with inoperable, somatostatin-receptor-positive midgut NETs who experienced progressive disease on standard doses of long-acting octreotide were randomly assigned to 4 doses of 177 Lu-dotatate every 8 weeks in 116 patients and long-acting octreotide 60 mg administered intramuscularly every 28 days in 113 patients. [18] In the 177 Lu-dotatate group, patients continued to receive long-acting octreotide at 30 mg after each infusion of 177 Lu-dotatate and then monthly after completing all 4 treatments. The study met the primary endpoint of progression-free survival (PFS). The estimated PFS rate at month 20 was significantly higher with 177Lu-dotatate, at 65.2% compared to 10.8%, which reached statistical significance. Among patients evaluated for radiographic response, 177 Lu-dotatate showed a significantly higher objective response rate of 18% compared to 3% for octreotide. The treatment-related serious adverse events are more common with 177 Lu-dotatate at 9% versus 1%. The most common adverse event in the 177 Lu-dotatate was nausea, possibly due to the amino acid infusions administered during therapy for renal protection. 177 Lu-dotatate is associated with hematologic toxicities due to irradiation of the bone marrow. The Netter-1 study reported mild degrees of thrombocytopenia in 25% of participants, lymphopenia in 18%, anemia in 14%, and leukopenia in 10% of participants that occurred 4 to 6 weeks after each infusion and resolved within 8 weeks. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Octreotide Scan -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cortistatins are another class of endogenously secreted neuropeptides that bind to SSTRs with high affinity. [4] [5] Somatostatin exerts its antiproliferative and antisecretory effects by binding to 1 of the 5 SSTR subtypes (SSTR1, SSTR2, SSTR3, SSTR4, and SSTR5), which are G-protein–coupled receptors (GPCRs). These receptors are widely distributed in the brain, pituitary gland, pancreas, thyroid, spleen, kidneys, gastrointestinal tract, blood vessels, peripheral nervous system, and immune cells. [6] SSTRs are most abundantly expressed in well-differentiated NETs, with SSTR2 showing the highest expression, followed by SSTR1, SSTR5, SSTR3, and SSTR4. [7] However, expression patterns can vary by tumor type.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Somatostatin -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the short half-life of endogenous somatostatin, synthetic analogs have been created that have a much longer half-life and are more useful in the management of the disease. These analogs include lanreotide, octreotide, seglitide, and vapreotide. While these medications can be extremely effective in reducing symptoms in patients, they are not 100% effective in all patients. Some patients only develop partial relief while others seem to be completely unaffected. In these patients who experience partial or zero relief, it appears the relative concentration and pattern of expression of the six somatostatin receptors to one another dictate the response to the synthetic analogs. This response is an area of potential research in treatment as the synthetic analogs must be developed to be more specific to the patient's unique somatostatin receptor pattern and receptor concentrations.

retrieved_docs.title: Octreotide Scan -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Contemporary oncological practice increasingly depends on octreotide scintigraphy for staging and detecting occult primary neuroendocrine neoplasms. This method is also relatively safe, as it avoids the adverse effects of using contrast in contrast-enhanced CT (CECT) and offers significantly lower radiation exposure. Newer studies highlight its potential in immunology, although further research is necessary to explore its broader applicability across various medical fields.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 640
retrieved_docs.content: Vasopressin and its analogues constitute a pharmacologic class that includes both desmopressin and terlipressin, each of which is a member of this group. These agents share structural similarity to vasopressin and act through related mechanisms, making them important therapeutic options within the vasopressin analogue category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 670
retrieved_docs.content: Somatropin and somatropin agonists constitute a therapeutic class of growth‑hormone–related agents, and this class includes the specific drugs mecasermin, sermorelin, somapacitan, and tesamorelin. Each of these agents functions as an agonist of the somatropin (human growth hormone) pathway, sharing a common mechanism of stimulating growth‑hormone receptors to promote anabolic and metabolic effects. By being members of the somatropin and somatropin agonists class, mecasermin, sermorelin, somapacitan, and tesamorelin are grouped together for clinical considerations such as indications, dosing strategies, and monitoring, facilitating a unified approach to managing conditions like growth‑hormone deficiency and related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 261
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES is positioned within the broader group of hypotalamické hormony (hypothalamic hormones) and serves as an overarching category that includes anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues. Both Somatropin and somatropin agonists and Thyrotropin Class in ATC are classified as subclasses of anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues. Within the Somatropin and somatropin agonists subclass, the specific therapeutic agents mecasermin, somapacitan and tesamorelin are recognized as members, reflecting their shared mechanism of action as growth‑hormone related compounds. Similarly, the Thyrotropin Class in ATC includes thyreotropin alfa as a member, representing a specific thyroid‑stimulating hormone analogue. This hierarchical organization links broad endocrine categories to individual hormone analogues and agents, facilitating clear pharmacological classification and supporting informed clinical decision‑making in endocrine therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 0 -- Community 3
retrieved_docs.content: targeting somatostatin receptors for neuroendocrine‑tumour imaging), fluorocholine (a fluorine‑containing imaging agent), technetium‑99m arcitumomab (derived from a monoclonal antibody), and CP 609754 (a technetium‑99m derivative of arcitumomab). The monoclonal antibody tanezumab belongs to the broader class of MONOKLONÁLNÍ PROTILÁTKY, while abciximab is a monoclonal antibody used to inhibit platelet aggregation. PEPTIDE‑DERIVED compounds traced to oligopeptide scaffolds—acetylphenylalanyl‑prolyl‑boroarginine, deltorphin I Ala(2)‑, and the antiviral telaprevir—illustrate the therapeutic and diagnostic utility of short peptide sequences. Collectively, these entities are organized by chemical class, mechanism of action, and clinical indication, providing a comprehensive framework for targeted patient care across viral, bacterial, fungal, oncologic and gastro‑intestinal domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2762
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308 is classified as a hormone antagonist, indicating that it functions by blocking hormone receptors. Atosiban is identified as a substance with a peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action and is also categorized as a hormone antagonist. These relationships show that both PD 134308 and atosiban share the functional property of antagonizing hormone receptors, a characteristic that is important for therapeutic approaches aimed at modulating hormone‑mediated physiological processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1124
retrieved_docs.content: Vasopressin and its analogues are classified as hormones of the posterior pituitary lobe, where they play key roles in water retention and vascular tone regulation. Within this group, terlipresin is recognized as a specific synthetic analogue, making it a member of the broader vasopressin‑analogue class. Understanding that vasopressin and its analogues, including terlipresin, originate from posterior pituitary physiology clarifies their mechanisms of action and informs their therapeutic application in conditions such as hypotension, variceal bleeding, and certain types of shock.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1420
retrieved_docs.content: Somatropin and somatropin agonists constitute a therapeutic class of synthetic growth‑hormone analogues, and the agents mecasermin, somapacitan and tesamorelin are all members of this class. These three drugs share a common mechanism of activating growth‑hormone receptors to stimulate insulin‑like growth factor‑1 production, thereby promoting growth and metabolic effects. Mecasermin is used primarily for treating growth‑failure conditions such as severe primary IGF‑1 deficiency, while somapacitan provides a long‑acting formulation for adult growth‑hormone deficiency, and tesamorelin is employed to reduce excess abdominal fat in patients with HIV‑associated lipodystrophy. Recognizing that mecasermin, somapacitan and tesamorelin belong to the somatropin and somatropin agonist class helps clinicians select appropriate growth‑hormone therapy based on specific patient needs and the pharmacokinetic profiles of each agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 471
retrieved_docs.content: Vasopressin and its analogues are classified as members of the terlipresin group, indicating that terlipresin is a synthetic analogue of the endogenous hormone vasopressin and that it belongs to the same pharmacologic class as other vasopressin‑based compounds. This relationship highlights the shared mechanism of action among these agents, which act on vasopressin receptors to produce vasoconstriction and antidiuretic effects, and underscores the relevance of terlipresin as a therapeutic option within the broader category of vasopressin analogues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 775
retrieved_docs.content: Antacids are contraindicated for use with the famotidine class of H₂‑receptor antagonists, indicating that concurrent administration should be avoided. Antacids, alkylating agents, and antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu (antidiuretic hormone antagonists) each belong to broader pharmacological categories defined by their molecular mechanisms, reflecting a hierarchical “has parent” relationship within drug action pathways. Antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu include agents such as tolvaptan, a vasopressin V₂‑receptor antagonist, illustrating that tolvaptan is a member of this drug class. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians prevent drug‑drug interactions, understand the mechanistic basis of therapy, and select appropriate medications for patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2955
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone (LHRH) agonists constitute a class of compounds that stimulate the gonadotropin‑releasing hormone receptor to modulate pituitary hormone release, and triptorelin is a specific agent that belongs to this LHRH‑agonist class. Recognizing triptorelin as a member of the LHRH‑agonist group is essential for understanding its mechanism of action and therapeutic applications in hormone‑dependent conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 163
retrieved_docs.content: sermorelin is also classified among somatropin and somatropin agonists. Collectively, these hierarchical classifications illustrate how hormone analogues, diagnostic agents, glucose‑lowering DPP‑4 inhibitors, and peptide‑derived radiopharmaceuticals are organized into functional therapeutic groups that guide their mechanisms of action, clinical applications in endocrine testing, hormone replacement, diabetes management, and targeted imaging, and their interrelationships in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2659
retrieved_docs.content: The substance with peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action, known as atosiban, functions as an inverse ISA, indicating that it antagonizes peptide hormone receptors while exhibiting inverse agonist properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1312
retrieved_docs.content: Atosiban is classified as a substance that exerts its effects through a peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action, placing it within the broader category of hormone antagonists. This classification highlights its role in blocking specific peptide hormone receptors, which is central to its therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2346
retrieved_docs.content: Antacids, alkylating agents, and antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu (antidiuretic hormone antagonists) are all grouped under a common parent category that describes their pharmacological effects at the molecular‑mechanism level. This classification indicates that each of these drug classes shares a foundational molecular basis for its therapeutic action, allowing clinicians and researchers to consider their mechanisms of action collectively when evaluating drug effects, potential interactions, and therapeutic strategies. Understanding that these diverse agents belong to the same overarching pharmacological‑mechanism hierarchy aids in a more integrated approach to patient care and drug development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 1
retrieved_docs.content: for tumour detection feature indium‑111‑pentetreotide (binds somatostatin receptors), indium‑111‑satumomab pendetide, fluorocholine, technetium‑99m‑arcitumomab (derived from monoclonal antibodies), technetium‑99m‑tilmanocept (dextran‑mannan conjugate used in the Lymphoseek kit for sentinel‑lymph‑node detection), and CP 609754, which has a narrower relationship to technetium‑99m‑arcitumomab. Monoclonal antibodies as a therapeutic class include abciximab and ibalizumab. Understanding the precise class membership, chemical scaffolds, mechanisms of action, and interaction profiles of these antivirals, antifungals, anticoagulants, proton‑pump inhibitors, and radiopharmaceuticals enables clinicians to select appropriate regimens, anticipate drug‑drug interactions, manage contraindications, and optimize patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1253
retrieved_docs.content: Anagrelide is a pharmacologic agent whose mechanism of action involves inhibition of phosphodiesterase enzymes; it functions as a phosphodiesterase inhibitor, a property that underlies its therapeutic effects in relevant clinical contexts.
